title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CARB Approves the Proposed Amendments after a Marathon Meeting
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(4th November â€“ 10th November)
  • A total of 175,000 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 1.05x times rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a fall of 18.10% WoW as 478,000 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased this week to $66.08/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 4.56%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 3.85% in price reaching $65.50/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 11.60% WoW; on 12th Nov, it was $76.00/MT. The benchmark price stood at $76.00/MT on 12th
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 2.85% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each increased by 5.80% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The average Type 1 prices witnessed a 1.05x increase, reaching $66.08/MT. The weekly average CA LCFS credit price climbed to $66.08/MT for Type 1 transfers, up 4.56% WoW, while the average LCFS spot credit price surged by 11.60% WoW to $76.00/MT on 12th November. Following a 12-hour meeting, CARB approved significant amendments to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) with a 12-2 vote. These changes include raising the carbon intensity reduction target to 30% by 2030, introducing mechanisms to adjust targets based on market performance automatically, and phasing out credits for biomethane in combustion engines. Instead, the focus will shift towards promoting its use in renewable hydrogen production, aligning with California's carbon neutrality goals. Additionally, stricter guidelines will be implemented to prevent deforestation, requiring independent certification to ensure sustainable sourcing of biofuel feedstocks. The meeting drew substantial public engagement, with environmental justice advocates expressing concerns over health impacts on vulnerable communities. Critics also highlighted the potential rise in fuel costs, though CARB staff clarified that outdated estimates of a 47-cent per gallon increase would be revisited with annual assessments. In collaboration with the California Energy Commission, CARB will conduct yearly evaluations of the amendments' effects on retail gasoline prices to ensure transparency. Following the final approval on 8th November, spot prices surged to $76.75 by 11th Novemberâ€”the highest level since 1st September 2023â€”demonstrating the marketâ€™s positive response to the anticipated impact of these regulatory updates, which are set to take effect in Q1 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_589533" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.56% WoW rise from $63.20 to $66.08 this week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices fell by 3.85% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 175,000 credits exchanged, recording a 1.05x times rise WoW this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had decreased by 18.1% WoW this week, to reach 478,000 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_589534" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 11.60% WoW, reaching $70.98/MT. On 12th Nov, the average spot price was $76.00/MT, which rose from $60.67/MT, the lowest point in October. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $76.00/MT as of 12th Nov, up from $61.38/MT as of 23rdÂ Oct.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_589535" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 45 trades were reported in the market, with three high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $80.00 for a transaction of Type 2 at a volume of 15,000 credits, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $58.00 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 5,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 50,000 credits in Type 1 were undertaken for prices of $63.00 and $70.00, and the smallest volume transfers of 2,500 credits, a Type 1 category, were undertaken for prices of $67.00 and $71.50.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_589536" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] October showed strong momentum, with volumes hitting 6.40 MMT, a significant 66.23% increase year-on-year compared to 3.85 MMT in October 2023. This marks the highest monthly transaction volume of 2024, surpassing all previous months. Meanwhile, Type 1 credit prices have maintained the upward trend that began in July, averaging $65.93 in October, demonstrating ongoing market resilience. For context, prices in October 2023 were around $70.12. Also, the average Type 1 price reached $66.08 as of 8th November.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

  [caption id="attachment_589539" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 04th Â Â Nov â€“ 10th Nov, total VPT recorded a 5.66% fall (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 2.85% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 5.80% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Attorney General Bailey Challenges Californiaâ€™s Proposed Amendment to Decimate Missouri Farms. Missouri Attorney General Andrew Bailey directed a letter to Californiaâ€™s Chair of California Air Resources Board (CARB) demanding it rescind its proposed amendment that would irreversibly harm Missouriâ€™s farmers and biofuels industry. â€œMissouriâ€™s agriculture and biofuels industries are vital to the success of our state, so I will do everything in my power to protect Missouri farmers,â€ said Attorney General Bailey.
  • Bay Area Air District and CARB Fine Valero Refining Co. $82 Million for Air Quality Violations. The Bay Area Air Quality Management District and California Air Resources Board (CARB) are announcing today a nearly $82 million penalty in a joint case to address significant air pollution violations by Valero Refining Co. at its Benicia refinery. This penalty is the largest ever assessed in the Air Districtâ€™s history. Over $64 million of these funds will be returned to the local community to finance projects aimed at reducing air pollution exposure, mitigating air pollution impacts and improving public health in areas surrounding the refinery.
  • South Dakota Voters Repeal CO2 Pipeline Law, Summit Carbon Solutions Moves Forward with Permitting Application. South Dakota voters on Nov. 5 rejected a law signed earlier this year that pertains to the development of carbon dioxide pipelines within the state. Summit Carbon Solutions has announced it will push forward with its proposed CO2 pipeline project and plans to apply for a permit later this month. In March, South Dakota Gov. Kristi Noem signed SB 201, a bill that created new statutory requirements for regulating linear transmission facilities, including CO2 pipelines. The law also allowed counties to impose a surcharge on certain pipelines companies and established a landowner bill of rights.
  • OPAL Fuels Begins Construction on California Renewable Natural Gas Production Facility. OPAL Fuels Inc. (Nasdaq: OPAL) announced today that construction has commenced on a new landfill-gas-to-RNG facility at the Kirby Canyon Landfill, located in Santa Clara County, California. The new facility will have an initial annual design capacity of approximately 0.66 million MMBtus and is 100% owned by OPAL Fuels. Approximately 5.1 million gas gallons equivalent (â€œGGEâ€) of RNG can be produced annually based on the design capacity of the plant.
